title: Built-in Roles, Custom Roles & Least Privilege in Practice
summary: Azure ships ~100 ready-made roles so you rarely invent your own. This page covers the four you'll use constantly, the dangerous-by-design one everybody overlooks, when to build a custom role, and how to actually practice least privilege.
analogy: Built-in roles are off-the-rack uniforms: Reader (visitor pass — look, don't touch), Contributor (worker — build anything but can't hire), Owner (manager — build AND hire), and User Access Administrator (HR — can't build, but controls who gets hired). Custom roles are the tailor, for when nothing off the rack fits.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Reader[Reader\nread-only, no data] --> Contributor[Contributor\nmanage all, NO access grants]
  Contributor --> Owner[Owner\nContributor + manage access]
  UAA[User Access Administrator\nmanage access only] -.no resource mgmt.-> Owner
  Owner --> Risk[Hands out access = high blast radius]
  Custom[Custom role] -->|only if no built-in fits| Fit[Exact Actions you need]
body:
## First principles

Defining a role from raw `Actions` for every team would be exhausting and error-prone. So Azure ships a large library of **built-in roles** covering the common jobs. The skill isn't writing roles — it's *choosing the smallest role that does the task*. That choice is where least privilege lives or dies.

## Why it exists

Most access needs are not unique. "Let them look but not change." "Let them manage resources but not give others access." "Let them control access but not touch the resources." Microsoft pre-built roles for these so you don't reinvent — and so auditors recognize them across every Azure tenant on earth.

## How it works — the four you must know

These are the **fundamental** general-purpose roles, applicable at any scope:

- **Reader** — view everything in scope, change **nothing**. No data access by default (reading resource metadata ≠ reading the data inside). The safe default for auditors, dashboards, and "I just need to look."
- **Contributor** — **manage** any resource in scope (create, modify, delete) but **cannot grant access to others** and cannot read data-plane data by default. This is the workhorse for builders. The deliberate gap — no access management — is a feature: a Contributor can't quietly escalate the team.
- **Owner** — Contributor **plus** the power to **manage access** (assign roles). Full control. Hand this out rarely and at the narrowest scope; an Owner can grant themselves or anyone else anything below that scope.
- **User Access Administrator** — the mirror image of Contributor: it can **manage access** (assign roles) but **cannot manage the resources themselves**. It exists to separate "who controls access" from "who builds things" — a clean separation-of-duties tool.

Notice the two axes: **manage resources?** and **manage access?** Reader = neither; Contributor = resources only; User Access Administrator = access only; Owner = both. That 2×2 is the whole mental model.

Beyond these four are ~100 **service-specific** roles that grant exactly what one service needs — *Virtual Machine Contributor*, *Storage Blob Data Reader*, *Key Vault Secrets User*, *Reader and Data Access*, and so on. Reach for the **narrowest service role** that covers the task before ever using a broad general one. Need to read blobs? *Storage Blob Data Reader*, not Contributor.

## Custom roles — when off-the-rack won't fit

When no built-in role matches — you need a precise slice, like "restart VMs but never delete them" — you author a **custom role**: your own JSON with exactly the `Actions`/`NotActions`/`DataActions` required, plus the scopes where it can be assigned. Custom roles are powerful but carry maintenance cost (you own them as Azure adds new operations), so use them only when a built-in genuinely doesn't fit. A tenant can hold up to 5,000 custom roles.

```bash
# Define a tightly-scoped custom role.
az role definition create --role-definition '{
  "Name": "VM Operator (restart only)",
  "Description": "Start/restart VMs, no create or delete.",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<sub-id>"]
}'
```

## Least privilege in practice

A usable checklist:

1. **Start from Reader.** Grant the weakest role first; widen only when something is actually blocked.
2. **Prefer service-specific over general.** *Storage Blob Data Reader* beats Contributor; *VM Contributor* beats Owner.
3. **Assign to groups, scope tightly.** Role on a group, at the resource group (not subscription), so grants are managed and contained.
4. **Hoard Owner.** Treat Owner like plutonium — few holders, narrow scope, and pair it with PIM so it's *eligible*, not standing.
5. **Separate duties.** Use User Access Administrator to let someone manage access without also being able to reshape the resources.
6. **Review regularly.** Permissions accrete ("privilege creep"). Periodic access reviews prune what's no longer needed.

## Gotchas

- **Owner sprawl is the classic failure.** It's the lazy fix for every 403, and every extra Owner multiplies blast radius. If you're reaching for Owner, you almost always want Contributor or a service role.
- **Contributor can't read data — on purpose.** A Contributor on a storage account still gets 403 reading blobs without a data role. Not a bug; control plane ≠ data plane.
- **User Access Administrator is quietly powerful.** It can't build anything, but it can grant *Owner* to others — an escalation path. Guard it like a privileged role (and put it behind PIM).
- **Custom roles are maintenance debt.** Exhaust built-ins first; an over-specific custom role can break when new resource operations appear that it doesn't include.
teachItBack: Azure ships about a hundred built-in roles, and four general ones cover most needs along two axes — can you manage resources, and can you manage access. Reader does neither, Contributor manages resources but not access, User Access Administrator manages access but not resources, and Owner does both, so hoard it. Always pick the narrowest service-specific role that does the job, assign it to a group at a tight scope, and only author a custom role when nothing built-in fits. Least privilege is a habit: start from Reader and widen reluctantly.

glossary.definition: Built-in role granting read-only view of resources in scope, with no data access by default.
Built-in role to fully manage resources but not grant access to others or read data-plane data by default.
Built-in role with full control: Contributor plus the ability to manage access (assign roles).
Built-in role that can manage access (assign roles) but not manage the resources themselves.
A user-defined role with an exact set of Actions/DataActions and assignable scopes, for when no built-in role fits.

sources.title: Dev docs — Understand Azure role definitions
